did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning, specifically transfer learning with pre-trained Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) including EfficientNetB0, DenseNet121, and ResNet50, to classify skin lesions (melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and benign nevi) from the HAM10000 dermoscopic dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the implementation and evaluation of pre-trained CNNs (EfficientNetB0, DenseNet121, ResNet50) using transfer learning on the HAM10000 dataset. It includes specific methodology sections (dataset details, model architecture, experimental setup), results with quantitative metrics (accuracy, precision, recall), and comparisons with existing works, which characterizes it as a research study rather than a review.

title: Skin Cancer Classification Using Pre-trained CNNs: A Transfer Learning Approach
journal: 2025 2nd International Conference on Next-Generation Computing, IoT and Machine Learning (NCIM)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of skin cancer classification by employing transfer learning with pre-trained Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), specifically EfficientNetB0, DenseNet121, and ResNet50. The researchers utilized the HAM10000 dermoscopic dataset, which contains 10,015 images across seven classes, to evaluate model performance. To mitigate class imbalance, the authors implemented advanced data augmentation techniques and a custom-weighted loss function. The models were tested on three dataset configurations: imbalanced 7-class, imbalanced 5-class, and a balanced augmented version. The results demonstrated that the proposed approach accurately differentiates between melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and benign nevi, achieving performance comparable to existing works while maintaining lower computational complexity.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the 7 classes in the HAM10000 dataset used in the study; 327 images.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion classes (melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, benign nevi, and other HAM10000 classes)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of skin lesions using pre-trained CNNs (EfficientNetB0, DenseNet121, ResNet50) on the HAM10000 dataset. The task involves distinguishing between discrete diagnostic categories (7-class, 5-class, and balanced augmented versions) rather than predicting continuous values. Metrics used include accuracy, precision, and recall, which are standard for classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (Human Against Machines)
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, divided into Part 1 (5000 images) and Part 2 (5015 images). All images were in JPEG format. The dataset is known for significant class imbalance, particularly with the Melanocytic Nevi (NV) class.

use.training: The base dataset was split into an 80:20 ratio (80% training, 20% testing) for the 7-class and 5-class imbalanced versions. For the DF and VASC classes, a 90:10 split was used. The training subset was augmented to generate 3500 images per class for the balanced version. Five-fold stratified cross-validation was employed.
use.validation: Used in a five-fold stratified cross-validation approach where each fold served as the validation set once while the model was trained on the remaining four subsets.
use.testing: The 20% portion of the initial split (or the held-out fold in cross-validation) was used for testing/evaluation.
use.other: The dataset was used in three configurations: imbalanced 7-class, imbalanced 5-class (excluding AKIEC and VASC), and a balanced augmented version.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To rigorously assess the performance and generalization of the models by ensuring each fold served as the validation set once while the model was trained on the remaining four subsets.
To mitigate the bias caused by class imbalance (specifically the dominance of the NV class) during model training.
Used as the primary metric to evaluate model performance, specifically to address the challenges posed by class imbalance in the dataset.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported in the paper.

HAM10000 Imbalanced 7 Classes.notes: For the base dataset (imbalanced 7-class), EfficientNetB0 achieved the highest average accuracy (83%) and accuracy (87%). DenseNet121 and ResNet50 had average accuracies of 82% and 81% respectively.

HAM10000 Imbalanced 5 Classes.notes: When two classes were removed (AKIEC and VASC), all models' performance improved. EfficientNetB0's average accuracy improved to 87% and overall accuracy jumped to 94%. EfficientNetB0 surpassed other models in this setup.

HAM10000 Balanced 7 Classes.notes: After augmenting the base dataset to create a balanced version, EfficientNetB0's average accuracy remained the same (83%) as the imbalanced 7-class setup, but its accuracy improved from 87% to 88%. In all different versions of the dataset, EfficientNetB0 surpassed other models.

Comparison with Other Works.notes: The proposed EfficientNetB0 model achieved 88% accuracy. Only the Balanced Ensemble model by Thwin et al. achieved higher accuracy (91%), but it requires more computational cost. The proposed method is noted as a good solution in terms of less computational complexity compared to other single models.

architecture_details.modifications: Modified for the specific task by adding new layers; fine-tuning approach used where pre-trained layers were initially frozen and later unfrozen

training_details.loss_function: Custom weighted loss function: L(y_true, y_pred) = - sum(w_i * y_true,i * log(y_pred,i + epsilon))
training_details.learning_rate_schedule: Dynamic schedule varying between 0.000001 and 0.001, combining piecewise linear and exponential schedules (linear ramp-up, constant plateau, exponential decay)
training_details.early_stopping: Triggered if validation accuracy failed to improve for 8 consecutive epochs
training_details.hardware: NVIDIA Tesla P100 GPU (16 GB HBM2, 3,584 CUDA cores)

classification_or_output_head.type: Custom dense layers added to pre-trained backbone
classification_or_output_head.classes: 7 classes (AKIEC, BCC, BKL, DF, MEL, NV, VASC) or 5 classes (excluding AKIEC and VASC) depending on dataset configuration

preprocessing_techniques: Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) to enhance image contrast
Removal of AKIEC and MEL classes due to poor image quality (quality control/filtering)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied random shifts, scaling, and rotations. Horizontal and vertical shifts were within 10% of image dimensions, scaling up to 20%, and rotations up to 20 degrees.
Introduced random distortions by applying elastic deformations to the image.
Flipped the image vertically to enhance variability in orientation.
Scaled the image randomly within a specified range, set to a factor of up to 10%.

conclusions: The proposed transfer learning approach using pre-trained CNNs (EfficientNetB0, DenseNet121, and ResNet50) effectively classifies skin lesions, accurately differentiating between melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and benign nevi.

EfficientNetB0 demonstrated superior performance compared to DenseNet121 and ResNet50 across all dataset configurations (imbalanced 7-class, imbalanced 5-class, and balanced augmented), achieving the highest average accuracy and accuracy scores.

The study successfully addressed class imbalance challenges in the HAM10000 dataset through advanced data augmentation techniques and a custom-weighted loss function, utilizing average accuracy as the primary metric to mitigate bias toward the majority class (Melanocytic Nevi).

The proposed EfficientNetB0 model achieved 88% accuracy on the balanced augmented dataset, offering comparable performance to existing works (such as Thwin et al.'s 91% accuracy) while maintaining lower computational complexity than ensemble methods.

A key limitation acknowledged by the authors is that the CNN models used are 'black box' models, meaning they lack interpretability regarding how decisions are made, which may hinder trust in clinical decision-making processes.
The authors conclude that incorporating Explainable AI (XAI) techniques is a necessary direction for future work to enhance trust in the model's decision-making process.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which exhibits severe class imbalance (Melanocytic Nevi comprises 6,705 of 10,015 images), potentially limiting generalizability to other populations or datasets.
The paper acknowledges that the CNN models used are 'black box' models lacking interpretability, which hinders trust in clinical decision-making; no Explainable AI (XAI) techniques were implemented.

External validation was not performed; the model was only evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset using five-fold stratified cross-validation, raising concerns about overfitting to this specific data distribution.

The authors removed images of poor quality from the AKIEC and MEL classes during preprocessing, which may introduce selection bias and affect the performance metrics for these critical cancer classes.

While the EfficientNetB0 model achieved 88% accuracy on the balanced augmented dataset, this is lower than the 91% accuracy reported by Thwin et al. using a VGG16 Balanced Ensemble, though the authors argue their method has lower computational complexity.

The study addresses class imbalance through data augmentation and a custom-weighted loss function rather than solely relying on standard metrics like overall accuracy, which can be misleading in imbalanced scenarios.

The dataset includes multi-source images which may introduce variability in image quality and lighting conditions, although the paper notes that pre-processing (CLAHE) helped mitigate some of these issues.
